questions.title: What is semantic HTML and why does it matter?
questions.shortAnswer: Semantic HTML uses tags that describe meaning (header, nav, article, section, footer) rather than just appearance, improving accessibility, SEO, and maintainability.
questions.detailedExplanation: Semantic elements convey the role of content to browsers, assistive tech, and search engines. Screen readers can build a meaningful outline, search engines weight content correctly, and developers can scan structure faster than reading a wall of divs.
questions.realWorldExample: A news site using <article>, <header>, <time>, and <aside> lets Google surface publish dates in SERPs and lets VoiceOver users jump straight to the main story.
bestPractices: Use one <h1> per page, then nested headings in order
Wrap navigation in <nav>, main content in <main>
questions.interviewTip: Mention the accessibility tree — interviewers love when you connect semantics to how AT actually consumes the DOM.

questions.title: Explain the difference between block, inline, and inline-block elements.
questions.shortAnswer: Block elements take full width and start on a new line; inline elements flow with text and ignore width/height; inline-block flows inline but accepts box dimensions.
questions.detailedExplanation: Display mode controls layout flow. Block participates in block formatting context; inline participates in line boxes; inline-block sits on the line but is sized like a block.
questions.realWorldExample: Buttons in a toolbar are usually inline-block so they wrap on the same line yet respect padding and explicit widths.
bestPractices: Use semantic tags; override display only when needed
Prefer flex/grid for layouts over display tweaks
commonMistakes: Setting width on a default inline <span>
Forgetting whitespace gaps between inline-block elements
questions.interviewTip: Bring up modern alternatives: flex/grid make this question mostly historical.

questions.title: What does the data-* attribute do?
questions.shortAnswer: data-* lets you attach custom data to elements, readable via element.dataset in JS without polluting standard attributes.
questions.detailedExplanation: Anything after data- becomes a camelCased key on dataset. It's valid HTML, ignored by browsers, and ideal for hooks into JS or CSS without misusing id/class.
questions.realWorldExample: Analytics libraries read data-track-id to capture clicks without coupling logic to specific class names.
bestPractices: Use for non-visual hooks, not for storing large state
commonMistakes: Storing JSON blobs that should live in state
Reading via getAttribute when dataset is cleaner
questions.interviewTip: Contrast it with aria-* — data-* is for app logic, aria-* is for assistive tech.

questions.title: How do <script>, <script async>, and <script defer> differ?
questions.shortAnswer: Plain script blocks parsing; async downloads in parallel and executes ASAP (order not guaranteed); defer downloads in parallel but executes after parsing in document order.
questions.detailedExplanation: Async is best for independent scripts (analytics). Defer is best for app code that depends on the DOM and on order. Modules default to defer.
questions.realWorldExample: Use defer for your main app bundle and async for a third-party tag that doesn't depend on your code.
bestPractices: Avoid mixing async + defer with dependencies
commonMistakes: Using async for code that needs the DOM
Putting blocking scripts at the top without defer
questions.interviewTip: Mention <script type="module"> implicitly defers — bonus knowledge points.

questions.title: What are ARIA roles and when should you avoid them?
questions.shortAnswer: ARIA roles describe element purpose for assistive tech. Avoid them when a native semantic element already provides the role — the first rule of ARIA is don't use ARIA.
questions.detailedExplanation: ARIA fills gaps in HTML semantics (custom widgets, dynamic content) but adds maintenance cost and can break if state isn't kept in sync. Native <button>, <nav>, <dialog> are always preferred.
questions.realWorldExample: A custom combobox built from divs needs role="combobox", aria-expanded, aria-controls — but a <select> needs none of it.
bestPractices: Keep aria-* state synced with visual state
commonMistakes: Forgetting to update aria-expanded on toggle
questions.interviewTip: Cite the five rules of ARIA — interviewers love named principles.
